structured_choices.data_source: De-identified chest CT radiology reports with structured metadata (acquisition date, scanner model, reporting radiologist, imaging center/site) from a hospital system spanning 4–5 years. Associated labels derived from: (1) pathology reports following biopsy or resection, (2) follow-up imaging reports within 12 months, or (3) clinician-adjudicated consensus review for ambiguous cases. Minimum cohort size: 50,000 studies with reports; labeled subset: ~10,000 studies (20% positive, 80% negative, reflecting typical prevalence of clinically significant incidental findings).
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: clinically significant incidental finding = any finding on initial chest CT that prompted diagnostic follow-up (biopsy, dedicated imaging, or clinical intervention within 12 months) AND was confirmed by pathology or follow-up imaging to require intervention or monitoring. Non-significant = no follow-up initiated, or follow-up returned benign/stable result. Only studies with documented follow-up disposition are labeled; unlabeled studies (majority) are excluded to avoid training on unreliable negatives.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with site stratification. Divide chronologically by acquisition date: months 1–36 (train, ~60% of labeled data), months 37–42 (validation, ~20%), months 43–48 (test, ~20%). Within each temporal window, stratify by imaging site/scanner to ensure each site appears in all three sets proportionally. This prevents data leakage of evolving reporting language, clinical protocols, and documentation artifacts while preserving the realistic distribution of site-level variation. Temporal ordering ensures the model is evaluated on its ability to generalize forward in time, not just to held-out random samples from the same distribution.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing and feature engineering transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling, imputation) are fit exclusively on the training set. The same fitted transformers are applied without modification to validation and test sets. No statistics, thresholds, or learned representations from validation or test data are used during training or model selection.
structured_choices.features: Raw input: full radiology report text (impression, findings, and technical sections). Engineered features: (1) transformer embeddings from a pretrained clinical BERT model (e.g., SciBERT or ClinicalBERT) fine-tuned on the training set; (2) structured metadata features: scanner model (one-hot encoded), imaging center/site (one-hot encoded), report length, day-of-week acquisition, radiologist seniority (if available). Structured features are fit (encoder parameters, statistics) on training data only. No features encoding the label, follow-up date, or future clinical documentation are included. Text preprocessing: lowercase, whitespace tokenization; no aggressive stemming to preserve clinical terminology precision.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based model: fine-tune a pretrained clinical language model (e.g., ClinicalBERT) on the training set by appending a two-layer feedforward classifier (hidden dim 256, dropout 0.2) on top of the [CLS] token embedding. Task: binary classification with cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters selected via validation set performance (see validation approach). Learning rate, number of fine-tuning epochs, and layer freezing strategy are tuned on validation data.
structured_choices.baseline: Bag-of-words TF-IDF baseline: extract TF-IDF features from report text (max 5,000 features, min document frequency 5, max document frequency 0.8) fit on training data only. Train a logistic regression classifier with L2 regularization on the same training set. Hyperparameter (regularization strength C) is tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same primary metric (AUROC) as the transformer model. Both baseline and model receive equivalent tuning effort. Both use identical structured metadata features (scanner, site, etc.). This ensures the comparison isolates the value of learned contextual embeddings vs. bag-of-words features, not differences in data availability or preprocessing.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC). Justification: In clinical triage, the goal is to rank-order studies by risk so that clinicians can set a threshold that balances sensitivity (catching significant findings) against specificity (avoiding false alarms). AUROC measures discrimination across all thresholds and is insensitive to class imbalance and threshold selection, making it appropriate for sparse labels and operational flexibility. The metric is measured on the held-out test set only.
secondary_metrics: Calibration error (expected calibration error, ECE, or Brier score) — clinicians must trust model confidence for safe threshold setting
Sensitivity at 90% specificity — operationally relevant: the alert rate when tuned to avoid overwhelming reviewers
Precision-recall AUC (AUPRC) — emphasizes performance on the positive class given class imbalance
Site-stratified AUROC — confirms the model does not rely on site-specific artifacts
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (months 37–42). Grid search or Bayesian optimization over learning rate, fine-tuning epochs, layer freezing, and dropout is conducted; the model checkpoint with the highest validation AUROC is selected. The validation set is used repeatedly during model selection. The test set is held completely untouched until final evaluation.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 43–48, stratified by site) is evaluated exactly once, after model selection is complete. Report: AUROC, AUPRC, calibration error, sensitivity at fixed specificity, and site-stratified performance. Confidence intervals (95% via bootstrap resampling on test data) are provided. No threshold tuning, feature re-engineering, or model retraining is performed after test evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: 1. Temporal split prevents leakage of evolving reporting practices and clinical protocol changes. 2. Site stratification across all splits ensures generalization across scanner/institution variability and prevents site-specific patterns from inflating model performance. 3. Structured metadata (scanner model, site) are included as explicit features, allowing the model to learn site effects rather than hide them. 4. Explicit test: compute AUROC within each site independently on test data; report if performance varies by >5 percentage points, indicating potential site confounding. 5. Label validation check: sample 100 positive and 100 negative test examples, have an independent clinician re-adjudicate labels to quantify potential label noise. 6. Ablation on test data: evaluate baseline model with and without structured metadata to confirm scanner/site features are not driving the gap. These controls do not establish causation (e.g., whether the model learns true clinical reasoning vs. reporting artifacts), but they rule out technical confounds and confirm external validity across sites and time.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF for Clinically Significant Incidental Findings Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses de-identified chest CT reports and metadata from a hospital system spanning 4–5 years. The raw dataset comprises approximately 50,000 studies; a subset of ~10,000 is labeled by linking reports to downstream pathology results, follow-up imaging, or clinician adjudication within 12 months of the initial scan. A study is labeled positive if follow-up confirmed the incidental finding was clinically significant (required intervention or close monitoring); studies with benign or no follow-up findings are labeled negative. Crucially, only studies with documented follow-up disposition are retained; unlabeled studies are excluded to avoid training the model on false negatives (findings that were never investigated because they were unreported or clinically dismissed).

This labeling approach introduces class imbalance (~20% positive) and selective labeling bias, both of which are realistic to the deployment setting. These are addressed through evaluation metrics (AUPRC, calibration) and stratification in the split strategy, not by rebalancing the training data.

### 2. Temporal and Site-Stratified Split

To prevent data leakage of reporting language evolution and clinical protocol drift, the data is split chronologically: training (months 1–36, ~6,000 labeled studies), validation (months 37–42, ~2,000 studies), test (months 43–48, ~2,000 studies). Within each temporal window, the data is stratified by imaging site and scanner model, ensuring each site appears proportionally in all three sets. This preserves the realistic distribution of multi-site variation while preventing future information from corrupting past training data.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The model operates on full radiology report text (impression, findings, and technical sections). Preprocessing transformers — including text tokenization, a fitted vocabulary, and any scaling or imputation for structured features — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data without refitting.

Engineered features include: (1) contextual embeddings from a pretrained clinical BERT model (e.g., ClinicalBERT), fine-tuned on training data; (2) structured metadata (one-hot encoded scanner model, imaging site, report length, acquisition day-of-week); (3) no features encoding the label, follow-up date, or future clinical events. Text is lowercased and whitespace-tokenized without aggressive stemming to preserve clinical terminology.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model fine-tunes a pretrained clinical language model by appending a two-layer feedforward classifier (hidden dimension 256, dropout 0.2) on the [CLS] token embedding, trained end-to-end with cross-entropy loss on the training set.

The baseline is a bag-of-words TF-IDF classifier: TF-IDF features (max 5,000 terms, min document frequency 5) extracted from reports and fit on training data, followed by logistic regression with L2 regularization. Critically, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (regularization strength C) are selected via grid search on validation data, and the baseline uses the same structured metadata features as the transformer model. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of learned embeddings, not differences in data or compute.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

Primary metric: AUROC on the held-out test set. AUROC is chosen because clinicians must rank-order studies by risk to set safe triage thresholds; AUROC measures discrimination across all thresholds and is robust to class imbalance and operational flexibility.

Secondary metrics: (1) expected calibration error — clinicians must trust model confidence; (2) sensitivity at 90% specificity — operationally relevant alert rate; (3) precision-recall AUC — emphasizes performance on sparse positive class; (4) site-stratified AUROC — rules out site-specific confounding.

Model selection uses validation AUROC; the test set is held untouched until final evaluation and is evaluated exactly once. Confidence intervals (95% via bootstrap resampling) are reported on all test metrics.

### 6. Confound Controls

Temporal splitting prevents leakage of evolving reporting and clinical protocols. Site stratification across all splits ensures generalization across scanners and institutions. Structured metadata are included as explicit features so that site effects are learned rather than hidden. On test data, AUROC is computed separately by site; variation >5 percentage points triggers investigation of site-specific artifacts. An independent clinician re-adjudicates 200 random test labels to quantify label noise. An ablation study (on test data) compares baseline performance with and without structured metadata, confirming that site features are not driving the model gap.

These controls do not establish that the model learns true clinical reasoning, but they confirm technical soundness and external validity across sites and time.
